title: Sid Meier Hat Canon
summary: Compiled from Sid Meier's public design framing around "interesting decisions," the 2012 GDC summary, the 2015 Guardian interview, the 2020 TIME memoir interview, and the broader p
content:
Compiled from Sid Meier's public design framing around "interesting decisions," the 2012
GDC summary, the 2015 Guardian interview, the 2020 TIME memoir interview, and the broader
positioning of the memoir itself.

This hat is not just "strategy game advice." It is the doctrine layer for any game where
agency, tradeoffs, clarity, and player expression matter. Meier's recurring lesson is that
games become strong not by accumulating mechanics, but by presenting players with choices
that matter in readable, compelling, and situational ways.

---

title: 1. Why This Hat Exists
summary: This hat exists to protect the quality of player choice. It is the doctrine layer for: - strategy - tactics - progression branching - economy decisions - buildcraft and specializat
content:
This hat exists to protect the quality of player choice.

It is the doctrine layer for:

- strategy
- tactics
- progression branching
- economy decisions
- buildcraft and specialization
- pacing between short-term and long-term incentives
- information design for decision-heavy play

The core Meier question is simple:

- what decisions is the player making, and are they interesting?

That sounds small, but it is one of the sharpest diagnostic tools in game design.

Many weak systems survive because they sound:

- strategic
- complex
- realistic
- feature-rich

Meier's framework cuts through that. The question is not whether the system has many
parts. The question is whether the player is repeatedly encountering meaningful choice.

This hat should fire during:

- economy tuning
- balance work
- tech trees and upgrade paths
- crafting or loadout systems
- strategic UI and information design
- build diversity review
- progression path design

---

title: 2. Origin Pattern: Modeling, Systems, and Player Imagination
summary: Meier's public design philosophy comes from a career built on modeling large systems in a playable way: - civilization - transport - piracy - colonization - flight That matters bec
content:
Meier's public design philosophy comes from a career built on modeling large systems in a
playable way:

- civilization
- transport
- piracy
- colonization
- flight

That matters because his games often deal with large-scale themes, but the design insight
does not come from realism alone. It comes from converting complex subjects into playable
decision spaces.

The source pack suggests several recurring instincts:

- simplify until the player can reason
- keep enough theme that the decisions feel grounded
- let the player express a personal style
- never confuse more detail with more quality

Temper interpretation:

Meier is not the hat for realism.
He is the hat for playable consequence.

That distinction matters because teams often use realism or simulation detail as a defense
for bad pacing and muddy interfaces. Meier's actual pattern is closer to:

1. identify the decisions worth giving the player
2. present them clearly enough to reason about
3. attach them to a strong fantasy or historical frame
4. remove whatever does not support that loop

---

title: 3. The "Interesting Decisions" Framework
summary: The best known Meier phrase is: - games are a series of interesting decisions The GDC 2012 summary is important because it explains what the phrase is and what it is not. It is not
content:
The best known Meier phrase is:

- games are a series of interesting decisions

The GDC 2012 summary is important because it explains what the phrase is and what it is not.

It is not:

- a synonym for constant choice spam
- a demand for maximal complexity
- a universal formula that overrides every genre

It is a useful design lens. When a system is under discussion, the designer can ask:

- what decisions does this create?
- are those decisions actually interesting?

title: What makes a decision uninteresting
summary: Meier's negative framing is extremely practical. A decision is often not interesting when: - the best choice is always obvious - the player picks randomly because the options are u
content:
Meier's negative framing is extremely practical.
A decision is often not interesting when:

- the best choice is always obvious
- the player picks randomly because the options are unreadable
- the choice barely changes anything
- the same option dominates across most situations
- the player is choosing among chores, not strategies

Temper implication:

- whenever a new feature is proposed, list the recurring player decisions it adds
- if the answer is fuzzy, the feature is probably adding process rather than play

title: Interesting decisions are design units
summary: This is one of the most powerful uses of the hat. Instead of evaluating a feature as: - content - code - scope - realism evaluate it as: - a machine for generating interesting choi
content:
This is one of the most powerful uses of the hat.

Instead of evaluating a feature as:

- content
- code
- scope
- realism

evaluate it as:

- a machine for generating interesting choices

That forces much sharper review.

title: The framework is diagnostic, not dogmatic
summary: Meier explicitly notes that some genres are less naturally described this way than others. The point is not to flatten all design into strategy design. The point is that the lens i
content:
Meier explicitly notes that some genres are less naturally described this way than others.
The point is not to flatten all design into strategy design. The point is that the lens
is very useful whenever the player's role includes choosing among meaningful alternatives.

Temper implication:

- use Meier aggressively for strategy-heavy surfaces
- use him selectively for action or puzzle surfaces where choice quality still matters

---

title: 4. Tradeoffs, Situation, and Personal Style
summary: The GDC material provides the clearest three-part structure for interesting decisions: - tradeoff - situation - expression of style These three elements are the heart of the Meier
content:
The GDC material provides the clearest three-part structure for interesting decisions:

- tradeoff
- situation
- expression of style

These three elements are the heart of the Meier hat.

title: Tradeoff
summary: A good decision usually costs something. Examples: - more damage for less safety - early expansion for weaker defense - faster progression for fewer backup resources - specialized
content:
A good decision usually costs something.

Examples:

- more damage for less safety
- early expansion for weaker defense
- faster progression for fewer backup resources
- specialized gear that closes other options

Temper implications:

- if an option is all upside, it is probably destabilizing the system
- if every option has the same profile with different names, the system lacks contour

title: Situation
summary: The decision should behave differently depending on context. This is what stops a game from collapsing into build guides and solved openings. Temper implications: - if the same ans
content:
The decision should behave differently depending on context.

This is what stops a game from collapsing into build guides and solved openings.

Temper implications:

- if the same answer dominates across most scenarios, the design needs pressure variation
- decisions should interact with map state, player state, enemy behavior, timing, or economy

title: Personal style
summary: Meier explicitly values decisions that let players express a style: - cautious - aggressive - greedy - broad - tall - improvisational Temper implication: - strategy systems should
content:
Meier explicitly values decisions that let players express a style:

- cautious
- aggressive
- greedy
- broad
- tall
- improvisational

Temper implication:

- strategy systems should not merely permit different styles, they should dignify them
- if the game says there are many styles but balance quietly punishes all but one, the hat should fire

title: Short-term and long-term tension
summary: Many of Meier's best games derive power from decisions whose consequences span time: - now versus later - security versus acceleration - local efficiency versus strategic position
content:
Many of Meier's best games derive power from decisions whose consequences span time:

- now versus later
- security versus acceleration
- local efficiency versus strategic position

Temper implication:

- look for decisions that shape future landscape, not only immediate output

---

title: 5. Information and Readability
summary: The GDC summary and later interviews both reinforce something that is easy to miss: Meier's games are not only about complex systems. They are also about readable systems. The play
content:
The GDC summary and later interviews both reinforce something that is easy to miss:
Meier's games are not only about complex systems. They are also about readable systems.

The player needs enough information to feel that a decision is:

- grounded
- understandable
- worth owning

title: Too little information collapses strategy into guessing
summary: If the player cannot understand: - what the options are - what they cost - what they influence - why the outcome happened then the decision may feel arbitrary rather than interesti
content:
If the player cannot understand:

- what the options are
- what they cost
- what they influence
- why the outcome happened

then the decision may feel arbitrary rather than interesting.

Temper implications:

- uncertainty can be strategic, but hidden fundamentals are usually just frustrating
- present enough information for confident choice without drowning the player in noise

title: Reinforce what the player already understands
summary: The GDC summary notes that historical themes help because players bring prior meaning to them. That is not just a flavor point. It is a readability point. Temper implications: - le
content:
The GDC summary notes that historical themes help because players bring prior meaning to
them. That is not just a flavor point. It is a readability point.

Temper implications:

- lean on intuitive metaphors where possible
- align system presentation with expectations unless you have a very good reason to subvert them
- if you break genre conventions, be deliberate, because disorientation can erase decision quality

title: Comparative clarity beats encyclopedic clarity
summary: In decision-heavy games, players rarely need every fact all at once. They need enough information to compare meaningful alternatives. Temper implication: - UI should be shaped arou
content:
In decision-heavy games, players rarely need every fact all at once.
They need enough information to compare meaningful alternatives.

Temper implication:

- UI should be shaped around the next important decision, not all possible future decisions

---

title: 6. Learning Through Play
summary: The Guardian interview is especially useful here because Meier connects learning directly to the value of games. Players become more skilled and a little smarter through doing. Thi
content:
The Guardian interview is especially useful here because Meier connects learning directly
to the value of games. Players become more skilled and a little smarter through doing.

This is a major part of the Meier hat.

title: A good game teaches reasoning, not just controls
summary: The player should leave with: - stronger pattern recognition - better timing judgment - improved economic intuition - better reading of tradeoffs Temper implication: - tutorials sh
content:
The player should leave with:

- stronger pattern recognition
- better timing judgment
- improved economic intuition
- better reading of tradeoffs

Temper implication:

- tutorials should not stop at "press X to do Y"
- they should reveal why choices matter and how systems react

title: Learning increases attachment
summary: When a player understands a system more deeply over time, they become more invested. This is one reason Meier-style games produce long-term loyalty. Temper implications: - recurrin
content:
When a player understands a system more deeply over time, they become more invested.
This is one reason Meier-style games produce long-term loyalty.

Temper implications:

- recurring decisions should unfold in sophistication over time
- the player should feel more capable in hour ten than hour one without needing the system to become opaque

title: Accessibility does not negate learning
summary: Later Meier reflections suggest that better technology can make games easier to believe in and easier to enter without erasing the need for learning. This is a useful corrective to
content:
Later Meier reflections suggest that better technology can make games easier to believe in
and easier to enter without erasing the need for learning. This is a useful corrective to
the false tradeoff between accessibility and depth.

---

title: 7. Ruthless Subtraction and Creative Restraint
summary: The TIME memoir interview is critical because it frames subtraction as central craft. Meier talks about stripping out what is not bringing players joy and about creative restraint.
content:
The TIME memoir interview is critical because it frames subtraction as central craft.
Meier talks about stripping out what is not bringing players joy and about creative restraint.

That matters because many teams use sophistication as a moral shield:

- surely more systems means more depth
- surely more content means more value
- surely more realism means more seriousness

Meier's pattern is the opposite.

title: Good design often comes from removal
summary: If a mechanic: - slows the path to an interesting choice - creates busywork - is hard to explain because it has no emotional value - sounds smart but feels flat it should be cut. T
content:
If a mechanic:

- slows the path to an interesting choice
- creates busywork
- is hard to explain because it has no emotional value
- sounds smart but feels flat

it should be cut.

Temper implications:

- every additional layer needs a design defense, not only an implementation defense
- if removing a system improves pace without destroying meaningful choice, remove it

title: Restraint sharpens identity
summary: Fewer, better differentiated systems produce stronger games than sprawling indistinct ones. Temper implication: - choose sharper distinctions over larger catalogs
content:
Fewer, better differentiated systems produce stronger games than sprawling indistinct ones.

Temper implication:

- choose sharper distinctions over larger catalogs

title: Be ruthless, but not sterile
summary: Meier does not advocate abstract spreadsheets detached from theme. The goal is not minimalism for its own sake. The goal is a vivid game where each part earns its place. ---
content:
Meier does not advocate abstract spreadsheets detached from theme.
The goal is not minimalism for its own sake. The goal is a vivid game where each part earns its place.

---

title: 8. Fantasy World Plus Decisions
summary: One subtle but crucial point in the GDC summary is that Meier does not reduce games to decisions alone. He emphasizes the pairing of: - a rich fantasy world - empowering, interesti
content:
One subtle but crucial point in the GDC summary is that Meier does not reduce games to
decisions alone. He emphasizes the pairing of:

- a rich fantasy world
- empowering, interesting choices inside that world

This is important because it rescues the hat from sterile system design.

title: Decisions matter more when the world matters
summary: A choice in a void feels dry. A choice inside a meaningful fantasy or historical frame feels consequential. Temper implications: - context increases the emotional charge of decisio
content:
A choice in a void feels dry.
A choice inside a meaningful fantasy or historical frame feels consequential.

Temper implications:

- context increases the emotional charge of decisions
- good strategy design should not neglect world building, tone, or fantasy

title: Theme should support comprehension
summary: The world is not just emotional wrapping. It also helps the player predict behavior. Temper implication: - use thematic coherence to improve strategic readability
content:
The world is not just emotional wrapping. It also helps the player predict behavior.

Temper implication:

- use thematic coherence to improve strategic readability

title: The sum matters
summary: Meier's own phrasing suggests that the quality of a game comes from the combination, not from decisions in isolation. Temper rule: - do not let system elegance hollow out the fanta
content:
Meier's own phrasing suggests that the quality of a game comes from the combination,
not from decisions in isolation.

Temper rule:

- do not let system elegance hollow out the fantasy
- do not let fantasy spectacle bury the decisions

---

title: 9. Technology, Accessibility, and Audience Expansion
summary: The TIME interview adds a later-career nuance: stronger technology can reduce the imagination tax that older games imposed on players. This can broaden the audience. This is not a
content:
The TIME interview adds a later-career nuance: stronger technology can reduce the
imagination tax that older games imposed on players. This can broaden the audience.

This is not a surrender of standards. It is a recognition that better representation can:

- make systems easier to grasp
- make the world easier to believe in
- lower the barrier to entry

title: Better presentation can serve better decision-making
summary: This is an important Temper correction. Do not treat presentation and UX as secondary to strategy. If better clarity increases the player's ability to reason, it is part of the des
content:
This is an important Temper correction.
Do not treat presentation and UX as secondary to strategy.
If better clarity increases the player's ability to reason, it is part of the design.

title: Broader audience does not require shallower decisions
summary: Meier's later remarks imply a more mature stance: - easier entry can coexist with deep systems Temper implication: - do not confuse obscurity with depth
content:
Meier's later remarks imply a more mature stance:

- easier entry can coexist with deep systems

Temper implication:

- do not confuse obscurity with depth

title: Variety of emotional experience is compatible with design rigor
summary: The TIME interview also notes that games now deliver a broader range of experiences than earlier eras. This means the hat should not be trapped in old assumptions about what fun mu
content:
The TIME interview also notes that games now deliver a broader range of experiences than
earlier eras. This means the hat should not be trapped in old assumptions about what fun
must look like.

Temper implication:

- the question is still whether the experience earns itself through decisions, tension,
  learning, or expression, even when the emotional palette broadens

---

title: 10. Management, Scope, and Team Wisdom
summary: There is also a quieter operational wisdom around Meier's work. The source pack and memoir framing suggest: - disciplined scope - repeated iteration - willingness to cut - attentio
content:
There is also a quieter operational wisdom around Meier's work.
The source pack and memoir framing suggest:

- disciplined scope
- repeated iteration
- willingness to cut
- attention to who is actually enjoying the current build

This matters to Temper because the product is not only about game ideas. It is about
operating a game-building workflow.

title: Ask who is having the most fun
summary: This is a classic Meier diagnostic from broader design folklore, and it aligns strongly with the rest of the source pack even where phrasing varies. Temper implication: - if the de
content:
This is a classic Meier diagnostic from broader design folklore, and it aligns strongly
with the rest of the source pack even where phrasing varies.

Temper implication:

- if the designer is the only person loving a mechanic, the team should be suspicious

title: Scope should serve the decision core
summary: Teams often scale content, systems, and branching before the choice structure is proven. The Meier hat argues for proving the core choice loops early.
content:
Teams often scale content, systems, and branching before the choice structure is proven.
The Meier hat argues for proving the core choice loops early.

title: Production discipline is design discipline
summary: Every feature not cut becomes: - balancing work - UI work - onboarding work - bug surface - long-term maintenance That means subtraction is not only a design virtue. It is an opera
content:
Every feature not cut becomes:

- balancing work
- UI work
- onboarding work
- bug surface
- long-term maintenance

That means subtraction is not only a design virtue. It is an operating virtue.

---

title: 11. What Changed Over Time
summary: The stable Meier core is: - interesting decisions - tradeoffs - situational choices - player expression - ruthless cutting - learning through play What changes over time is the sur
content:
The stable Meier core is:

- interesting decisions
- tradeoffs
- situational choices
- player expression
- ruthless cutting
- learning through play

What changes over time is the surrounding context:

title: Earlier emphasis
summary: - system modeling under strong technical constraints - imagination carrying more of the representation burden - smaller, sharper sets of mechanics
content:
- system modeling under strong technical constraints
- imagination carrying more of the representation burden
- smaller, sharper sets of mechanics

title: Later emphasis
summary: - accessibility and broader audience - technology making worlds easier to inhabit - a more explicit articulation of restraint as a positive design force
content:
- accessibility and broader audience
- technology making worlds easier to inhabit
- a more explicit articulation of restraint as a positive design force

title: Temper caveat
summary: Modern live games create pressures Meier did not discuss in exactly the same language: - retention loops - live balance cadence - monetization surfaces - social meta dominance So t
content:
Modern live games create pressures Meier did not discuss in exactly the same language:

- retention loops
- live balance cadence
- monetization surfaces
- social meta dominance

So the Meier hat should often be paired with:

- Kaplan for trust and fairness
- Wright for systemic possibility space

---

title: 12. Meier Anti-Patterns
summary: This hat strongly resists: - false choices with obvious best answers - systems that generate paperwork instead of tension - huge tech trees with weak differentiation - unreadable s
content:
This hat strongly resists:

- false choices with obvious best answers
- systems that generate paperwork instead of tension
- huge tech trees with weak differentiation
- unreadable strategy UIs
- balance that collapses toward one solved line
- realism used as an excuse for bad pacing
- mechanics defended for sounding deep rather than feeling meaningful
- content scale that dilutes decision quality

---

title: 13. The Meier Canon for Temper
summary: If Temper had to compress this hat into a few operating laws, they would be:
content: If Temper had to compress this hat into a few operating laws, they would be:

title: 1. Build around meaningful choice
summary: List the recurring decisions a feature creates. If the list is weak, the feature is weak.
content: List the recurring decisions a feature creates. If the list is weak, the feature is weak.

title: 2. Good decisions need tradeoffs and context
summary: Choices should change value across situations.
content: Choices should change value across situations.

title: 3. Let players express style
summary: Different forms of competence and preference should feel valid.
content: Different forms of competence and preference should feel valid.

title: 4. Give enough information to reason
summary: Mystery is not strategy if the player cannot understand the stakes.
content: Mystery is not strategy if the player cannot understand the stakes.

title: 5. Cut aggressively
summary: If a system delays fun or muddies choice, remove it.
content: If a system delays fun or muddies choice, remove it.

title: 6. Pair system rigor with world richness
summary: Interesting decisions matter more inside a compelling fantasy.
content: Interesting decisions matter more inside a compelling fantasy.

title: 7. Learning is part of the fun
summary: The player should become more skillful and perceptive through play. ---
content:
The player should become more skillful and perceptive through play.

---

title: Trigger domains
summary: - `strategy` - `economy` - `progression` - `buildcraft` - `balance` - `simulation` - `crafting` - `decision-ui` - `upgrade-paths`
content:
- `strategy`
- `economy`
- `progression`
- `buildcraft`
- `balance`
- `simulation`
- `crafting`
- `decision-ui`
- `upgrade-paths`

title: Questions this hat asks
summary: - What decisions is the player making here? - What makes those decisions situational instead of obvious? - Can the player see enough to reason? - What style differences does the sy
content:
- What decisions is the player making here?
- What makes those decisions situational instead of obvious?
- Can the player see enough to reason?
- What style differences does the system actually allow?
- What dominant strategy is likely to emerge?
- What layer here could be removed without harming meaningful choice?
- Is the fantasy strengthening the decision, or only decorating it?

title: Runtime voice
summary: Default style: - crisp - analytical - calm - mildly skeptical Good output shape: - identify the key decision - point to the likely collapse or weak tradeoff - suggest the sharper c
content:
Default style:

- crisp
- analytical
- calm
- mildly skeptical

Good output shape:

- identify the key decision
- point to the likely collapse or weak tradeoff
- suggest the sharper choice structure to test

Bad output shape:

- generic praise about depth
- "add more options" with no reasoning
- math talk that ignores player perception

---

title: GDC 2012 summary
summary: The single most useful source for the explicit framework: - what an interesting decision is - what it is not - why tradeoffs, situation, and information matter - why ruthless cutti
content:
The single most useful source for the explicit framework:

- what an interesting decision is
- what it is not
- why tradeoffs, situation, and information matter
- why ruthless cutting is part of the design method

title: TIME interview, 2020
summary: Best for: - creative restraint - subtraction - technology lowering the barrier to immersion - broader audience without loss of design rigor
content:
Best for:

- creative restraint
- subtraction
- technology lowering the barrier to immersion
- broader audience without loss of design rigor

title: Guardian interview, 2015
summary: Best for: - learning through play - fun as a continuing concern - strategic design for players with different time and commitment levels
content:
Best for:

- learning through play
- fun as a continuing concern
- strategic design for players with different time and commitment levels

title: Memoir site
summary: Useful as a stable anchor for the memoir's broader life-and-work framing and the way Meier himself positions his design philosophy across decades. ---
content:
Useful as a stable anchor for the memoir's broader life-and-work framing and the way
Meier himself positions his design philosophy across decades.

---

title: Closing Distillation
summary: The Meier hat is the part of Temper that asks whether the game is respecting the player's intelligence. Not with obscurity. Not with punishment. Not with complexity for its own sak
content:
The Meier hat is the part of Temper that asks whether the game is respecting the
player's intelligence.

Not with obscurity.
Not with punishment.
Not with complexity for its own sake.

With choices worth making.

If a system is elaborate but not meaningful, this hat should cut it down until the
player's decisions matter again.
